item.title: How to Use Flat-Bottomed Potentials in GROMACS
item.description: Flat bottomed potentials (FBPs) are a unique (and powerful) type of restraint we can apply to specific particles in a molecular dynamics simulation. In short, we define a fixed geometric region within the simulation box to which certain atoms will either be attracted or repelled. They’re also a little tricky to understand from a technical perspective. In this post, I’ll briefly discuss the maths, how to implement an FBP in the topology and restraints files, and finish up with some practical notes for usage.

About Me Link to heading I’m an Australian PhD Student in computational biophysics at the Molecular Microbology and Structural Biochemistry (MMSB) unit of the Centre national de la recherche scientifique (CNRS), **based in Lyon, France. Specifically, I work on atomistic and coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations of lipid droplets (and more). The biogenesis of these tiny organelles, fundamental to energy metabolism within the cell, is extremely difficult to study expexperimentally, due to their highly fluid nature and the fact that they often only consists of a few hundred molecules when they are first created.
